title: LTE (telecommunication)

infobox.value: (**LTE**) is a [standard](/wiki/Technical_standard "Technical standard") for [wireless broadband](/wiki/Wireless_broadband "Wireless broadband") communication for [cellular](/wiki/Cellular_network "Cellular network") mobile devices and data terminals. It is considered to be a "transitional" [4G](/wiki/4G "4G") technology,[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1) and is therefore also referred to as **3.95G** as a step above [3G](/wiki/3G "3G").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

and has been marketed as **4G LTE** and **Advanced 4G**;[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] but the original version did not meet the technical criteria of a [4G](/wiki/4G "4G") wireless service, as specified in the 3GPP Release 8 and 9 document series for [LTE Advanced](/wiki/LTE_Advanced "LTE Advanced"). The requirements were set forth by the [ITU-R](/wiki/ITU-R "ITU-R") organisation in the [IMT Advanced](/wiki/IMT_Advanced "IMT Advanced") specification; but, because of market pressure and the significant advances that [WiMAX](/wiki/WiMAX "WiMAX"), [Evolved High Speed Packet Access](/wiki/Evolved_High_Speed_Packet_Access "Evolved High Speed Packet Access"), and LTE bring to the original 3G technologies, ITU-R later decided that LTE and the aforementioned technologies can be called 4G technologies.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The LTE Advanced standard formally satisfies the ITU-R requirements for being considered IMT-Advanced.[[7]](#cite_note-7) To differentiate LTE Advanced and [WiMAX-Advanced](/wiki/WiMAX-Advanced "WiMAX-Advanced") from current[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] 4G technologies, ITU has defined the latter as "True 4G".[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Standard for wireless broadband communication for mobile devices

"Long-term evolution" redirects here. For the biological concept, see [Evolution](/wiki/Evolution "Evolution") and [E. coli long-term evolution experiment](/wiki/E._coli_long-term_evolution_experiment "E. coli long-term evolution experiment").

In [telecommunications](/wiki/Telecommunications "Telecommunications"), **long-term evolution** (**LTE**) is a [standard](/wiki/Technical_standard "Technical standard") for [wireless broadband](/wiki/Wireless_broadband "Wireless broadband") communication for [cellular](/wiki/Cellular_network "Cellular network") mobile devices and data terminals. It is considered to be a "transitional" [4G](/wiki/4G "4G") technology,[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1) and is therefore also referred to as **3.95G** as a step above [3G](/wiki/3G "3G").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

LTE is based on the [2G](/wiki/2G "2G") [GSM](/wiki/GSM "GSM")/[EDGE](/wiki/Enhanced_Data_Rates_for_GSM_Evolution "Enhanced Data Rates for GSM Evolution") and 3G [UMTS](/wiki/UMTS "UMTS")/[HSPA](/wiki/High_Speed_Packet_Access "High Speed Packet Access") standards. It improves on those standards' capacity and speed by using a different radio interface and core network improvements.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) LTE is the upgrade path for carriers with both GSM/UMTS networks and [CDMA2000](/wiki/CDMA2000 "CDMA2000") networks. LTE has been succeeded by [LTE Advanced](/wiki/LTE_Advanced "LTE Advanced"), which is officially defined as a "true" 4G technology[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) and also named "LTE+".

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LTE_(telecommunication)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

The standard is developed by the [3GPP](/wiki/3GPP "3GPP") (3rd Generation Partnership Project) and is specified in its Release 8 document series, with minor enhancements described in Release 9. LTE is also called **3.95G** and has been marketed as **4G LTE** and **Advanced 4G**;[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] but the original version did not meet the technical criteria of a [4G](/wiki/4G "4G") wireless service, as specified in the 3GPP Release 8 and 9 document series for [LTE Advanced](/wiki/LTE_Advanced "LTE Advanced"). The requirements were set forth by the [ITU-R](/wiki/ITU-R "ITU-R") organisation in the [IMT Advanced](/wiki/IMT_Advanced "IMT Advanced") specification; but, because of market pressure and the significant advances that [WiMAX](/wiki/WiMAX "WiMAX"), [Evolved High Speed Packet Access](/wiki/Evolved_High_Speed_Packet_Access "Evolved High Speed Packet Access"), and LTE bring to the original 3G technologies, ITU-R later decided that LTE and the aforementioned technologies can be called 4G technologies.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The LTE Advanced standard formally satisfies the ITU-R requirements for being considered IMT-Advanced.[[7]](#cite_note-7) To differentiate LTE Advanced and [WiMAX-Advanced](/wiki/WiMAX-Advanced "WiMAX-Advanced") from current[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] 4G technologies, ITU has defined the latter as "True 4G".[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LTE_(telecommunication)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Overview")]

LTE stands for Long-Term Evolution[[9]](#cite_note-9) and is a registered trademark owned by [ETSI](/wiki/ETSI "ETSI") (European Telecommunications Standards Institute) for the wireless [data communications](/wiki/Data_communication "Data communication") technology and development of the GSM/UMTS standards. However, other nations and companies do play an active role in the LTE project. The goal of LTE was to increase the capacity and speed of wireless data networks using new [DSP](/wiki/Digital_signal_processing "Digital signal processing") (digital signal processing) techniques and modulations that were developed around the turn of the millennium. A further goal was the redesign and simplification of the [network architecture](/wiki/Network_architecture "Network architecture") to an [IP](/wiki/Internet_Protocol "Internet Protocol")-based system with significantly reduced transfer [latency](/wiki/Network_latency "Network latency") compared with the [3G](/wiki/3G "3G") architecture. The LTE wireless interface is incompatible with [2G](/wiki/2G "2G") and 3G networks so it must be operated on a separate [radio spectrum](/wiki/Radio_spectrum "Radio spectrum").

The idea of LTE was first proposed in 1998, with the use of the [COFDM](/wiki/COFDM "COFDM") radio access technique to replace the [CDMA](/wiki/CDMA "CDMA") and studying its Terrestrial use in the L band at 1428 MHz (TE)
In 2004 by Japan's [NTT Docomo](/wiki/NTT_Docomo "NTT Docomo"), with studies on the standard officially commenced in 2005.[[10]](#cite_note-rel8wp-10) In May 2007, the LTE/[SAE](/wiki/System_Architecture_Evolution "System Architecture Evolution") Trial Initiative (LSTI) alliance was founded as a global collaboration between vendors and operators with the goal of verifying and promoting the new standard in order to ensure the global introduction of the technology as quickly as possible.[[11]](#cite_note-lsti-3gpp-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The LTE standard was finalized in December 2008, and the first publicly available LTE service was launched by [TeliaSonera](/wiki/TeliaSonera "TeliaSonera") in [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo") and [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm") on December 14, 2009, as a data connection with a USB modem. The LTE services were launched by major North American carriers as well, with the Samsung SCH-r900 being the world's first LTE Mobile phone starting on September 21, 2010,[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) and Samsung Galaxy Indulge being the world's first LTE smartphone starting on February 10, 2011,[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) both offered by [MetroPCS](/wiki/MetroPCS "MetroPCS"), and the [HTC ThunderBolt](/wiki/HTC_ThunderBolt "HTC ThunderBolt") offered by Verizon starting on March 17 being the second LTE smartphone to be sold commercially.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) In Canada, [Rogers Wireless](/wiki/Rogers_Wireless "Rogers Wireless") was the first to launch LTE network on July 7, 2011, offering the Sierra Wireless AirCard 313U USB mobile broadband modem, known as the "LTE Rocket stick" then followed closely by mobile devices from both HTC and Samsung.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Initially, CDMA operators planned to upgrade to rival standards called [UMB](/wiki/Ultra_Mobile_Broadband "Ultra Mobile Broadband") and [WiMAX](/wiki/WiMAX "WiMAX"), but major CDMA operators (such as [Verizon](/wiki/Verizon_Wireless "Verizon Wireless"), [Sprint](/wiki/Sprint_Corporation "Sprint Corporation") and [MetroPCS](/wiki/MetroPCS "MetroPCS") in the United States, [Bell](/wiki/Bell_Mobility "Bell Mobility") and [Telus](/wiki/Telus_Mobility "Telus Mobility") in Canada, [au by KDDI](/wiki/Au_(mobile_phone_company) "Au (mobile phone company)") in Japan, [SK Telecom](/wiki/SK_Telecom "SK Telecom") in South Korea and [China Telecom](/wiki/China_Telecom "China Telecom")/[China Unicom](/wiki/China_Unicom "China Unicom") in China) have announced instead they intend to migrate to LTE. The next version of LTE is [LTE Advanced](/wiki/LTE_Advanced "LTE Advanced"), which was standardized in March 2011.[[20]](#cite_note-LTE_End-to-End_System_Description-20) Services commenced in 2013.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Additional evolution known as [LTE Advanced Pro](/wiki/LTE_Advanced_Pro "LTE Advanced Pro") have been approved in year 2015.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The LTE specification provides downlink peak rates of 300 Mbit/s, uplink peak rates of 75 Mbit/s and [QoS](/wiki/Quality_of_service "Quality of service") provisions permitting a transfer [latency](/wiki/Network_latency "Network latency") of less than 5 [ms](/wiki/Millisecond "Millisecond") in the [radio access network](/wiki/E-UTRA "E-UTRA"). LTE has the ability to manage fast-moving mobiles and supports multi-cast and broadcast streams. LTE supports scalable carrier [bandwidths](/wiki/Bandwidth_(signal_processing) "Bandwidth (signal processing)"), from 1.4 [MHz](/wiki/MHz "MHz") to 20 MHz and supports both [frequency division duplexing](/wiki/Frequency_division_duplexing "Frequency division duplexing") (FDD) and [time-division duplexing](/wiki/Time-division_duplex "Time-division duplex") (TDD). The IP-based network architecture, called the [Evolved Packet Core](/wiki/Evolved_Packet_Core "Evolved Packet Core") (EPC) designed to replace the [GPRS Core Network](/wiki/GPRS_Core_Network "GPRS Core Network"), supports seamless [handovers](/wiki/Handover "Handover") for both voice and data to cell towers with older network technology such as [GSM](/wiki/GSM "GSM"), [UMTS](/wiki/UMTS "UMTS") and [CDMA2000](/wiki/CDMA2000 "CDMA2000").[[23]](#cite_note-EricssonWhitePaper-23) The simpler architecture results in lower operating costs (for example, each [E-UTRA](/wiki/E-UTRA "E-UTRA") cell will support up to four times the data and voice capacity supported by HSPA[[24]](#cite_note-Motorola_White_Paper:_Long_Term_Evolution_(LTE)-24)).

Because [LTE frequencies and bands](/wiki/LTE_frequency_bands "LTE frequency bands") differ from country to country, only multi-band phones can use LTE in all countries where it is supported.

## LTE-TDD and LTE-FDD

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LTE_(telecommunication)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: LTE-TDD and LTE-FDD")]

**Long-Term Evolution Time-Division Duplex** (**LTE-TDD**), also referred to as **TDD LTE**, is a [4G](/wiki/4G "4G") telecommunications technology and standard co-developed by an international coalition of companies, including [China Mobile](/wiki/China_Mobile "China Mobile"), [Datang Telecom](/wiki/Datang_Telecom "Datang Telecom"), [Huawei](/wiki/Huawei "Huawei"), [ZTE](/wiki/ZTE "ZTE"), [Nokia Solutions and Networks](/wiki/Nokia_Solutions_and_Networks "Nokia Solutions and Networks"), [Qualcomm](/wiki/Qualcomm "Qualcomm"), [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung"), and [ST-Ericsson](/wiki/ST-Ericsson "ST-Ericsson"). It is one of the two mobile data transmission technologies of the Long-Term Evolution (LTE) technology standard, the other being **Long-Term Evolution Frequency-Division Duplex** (**LTE-FDD**). While some companies refer to LTE-TDD as "TD-LTE" for familiarity with [TD-SCDMA](/wiki/TD_SCDMA "TD SCDMA"), there is no reference to that abbreviation anywhere in the 3GPP specifications.[[74]](#cite_note-ChinaDaily13-74)[[75]](#cite_note-Kan11-75)[[76]](#cite_note-Qing11-76)

There are two major differences between LTE-TDD and LTE-FDD: how data is uploaded and downloaded, and what frequency spectra the networks are deployed in. While LTE-FDD uses paired frequencies to upload and download data,[[77]](#cite_note-Meyer13-77) LTE-TDD uses a single frequency, alternating between uploading and downloading data through time.[[78]](#cite_note-Jones12-78)[[79]](#cite_note-Kim13-79) The ratio between uploads and downloads on a LTE-TDD network can be changed dynamically, depending on whether more data needs to be sent or received.[[80]](#cite_note-RadioElectronicsA-80) LTE-TDD and LTE-FDD also operate on different frequency bands,[[81]](#cite_note-OSullivan10-81) with LTE-TDD working better at higher frequencies, and LTE-FDD working better at lower frequencies.[[82]](#cite_note-Taylor12-82) Frequencies used for LTE-TDD range from 1850 MHz to 3800 MHz, with several different bands being used.[[83]](#cite_note-RadioElectronicsB-83) The LTE-TDD spectrum is generally cheaper to access, and has less traffic.[[81]](#cite_note-OSullivan10-81) Further, the bands for LTE-TDD overlap with those used for [WiMAX](/wiki/WiMAX "WiMAX"), which can easily be upgraded to support LTE-TDD.[[81]](#cite_note-OSullivan10-81)

Despite the differences in how the two types of LTE handle data transmission, LTE-TDD and LTE-FDD share 90 percent of their core technology, making it possible for the same chipsets and networks to use both versions of LTE.[[81]](#cite_note-OSullivan10-81)[[84]](#cite_note-Wireless13-84) A number of companies produce dual-mode chips or mobile devices, including [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung") and [Qualcomm](/wiki/Qualcomm "Qualcomm"),[[85]](#cite_note-Costello13-85)[[86]](#cite_note-Digit13-86) while operators [CMHK](/wiki/CMHK "CMHK") and Hi3G Access have developed dual-mode networks in Hong Kong and Sweden, respectively.[[87]](#cite_note-TTMag12-87)

### History of LTE-TDD

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LTE_(telecommunication)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: History of LTE-TDD")]

The creation of LTE-TDD involved a coalition of international companies that worked to develop and test the technology.[[88]](#cite_note-Min13-88) [China Mobile](/wiki/China_Mobile "China Mobile") was an early proponent of LTE-TDD,[[81]](#cite_note-OSullivan10-81)[[89]](#cite_note-Curtis12-89) along with other companies like [Datang Telecom](/wiki/Datang_Telecom "Datang Telecom")[[88]](#cite_note-Min13-88) and [Huawei](/wiki/Huawei "Huawei"), which worked to deploy LTE-TDD networks, and later developed technology allowing LTE-TDD equipment to operate in [white spaces](/wiki/White_spaces_(radio) "White spaces (radio)")—frequency spectra between broadcast TV stations.[[75]](#cite_note-Kan11-75)[[90]](#cite_note-Wood11-90) [Intel](/wiki/Intel "Intel") also participated in the development, setting up a LTE-TDD interoperability lab with Huawei in China,[[91]](#cite_note-GlobalTelecoms12-91) as well as [ST-Ericsson](/wiki/ST-Ericsson "ST-Ericsson"),[[81]](#cite_note-OSullivan10-81) Nokia,[[81]](#cite_note-OSullivan10-81) and Nokia Siemens (now [Nokia Solutions and Networks](/wiki/Nokia_Solutions_and_Networks "Nokia Solutions and Networks")),[[75]](#cite_note-Kan11-75) which developed LTE-TDD base stations that increased capacity by 80 percent and coverage by 40 percent.[[92]](#cite_note-Sakr11-92) [Qualcomm](/wiki/Qualcomm "Qualcomm") also participated, developing the world's first multi-mode chip, combining both LTE-TDD and LTE-FDD, along with HSPA and EV-DO.[[86]](#cite_note-Digit13-86) Accelleran, a Belgian company, has also worked to build small cells for LTE-TDD networks.[[93]](#cite_note-Fitchard13b-93)

Trials of LTE-TDD technology began as early as 2010, with [Reliance Industries](/wiki/Reliance_Industries "Reliance Industries") and Ericsson India conducting field tests of LTE-TDD in [India](/wiki/India "India"), achieving 80 megabit-per second download speeds and 20 megabit-per-second upload speeds.[[94]](#cite_note-IndianExpress10-94) By 2011, China Mobile began trials of the technology in six cities.[[75]](#cite_note-Kan11-75)

Although initially seen as a technology utilized by only a few countries, including China and India,[[95]](#cite_note-Nokia10-95) by 2011 international interest in LTE-TDD had expanded, especially in Asia, in part due to LTE-TDD's lower cost of deployment compared to LTE-FDD.[[75]](#cite_note-Kan11-75) By the middle of that year, 26 networks around the world were conducting trials of the technology.[[76]](#cite_note-Qing11-76) The Global LTve (GTI) was also started in 2011, with founding partners China Mobile, [Bharti Airtel](/wiki/Bharti_Airtel "Bharti Airtel"), [SoftBank Mobile](/wiki/SoftBank_Mobile "SoftBank Mobile"), [Vodafone](/wiki/Vodafone "Vodafone"), [Clearwire](/wiki/Clearwire "Clearwire"), Aero2 and [E-Plus](/wiki/E-Plus "E-Plus").[[96]](#cite_note-Telecoms-96) In September 2011, Huawei announced it would partner with Polish mobile provider Aero2 to develop a combined LTE-TDD and LTE-FDD network in Poland,[[97]](#cite_note-ComputerNews-97) and by April 2012, [ZTE Corporation](/wiki/ZTE "ZTE") had worked to deploy trial or commercial LTE-TDD networks for 33 operators in 19 countries.[[87]](#cite_note-TTMag12-87) In late 2012, Qualcomm worked extensively to deploy a commercial LTE-TDD network in India, and partnered with Bharti Airtel and Huawei to develop the first multi-mode LTE-TDD smartphone for India.[[86]](#cite_note-Digit13-86)

In [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), SoftBank Mobile launched LTE-TDD services in February 2012 under the name **Advanced eXtended Global Platform** (AXGP), and marketed as SoftBank 4G ([ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/SoftBank_4G "ja:SoftBank 4G")). The AXGP band was previously used for [Willcom](/wiki/Willcom "Willcom")'s [PHS](/wiki/Personal_Handy-phone_System "Personal Handy-phone System") service, and after PHS was discontinued in 2010 the PHS band was re-purposed for AXGP service.[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99)

In the U.S., Clearwire planned to implement LTE-TDD, with chip-maker Qualcomm agreeing to support Clearwire's frequencies on its multi-mode LTE chipsets.[[100]](#cite_note-Goldstein12-100) With [Sprint's](/wiki/Sprint_Corporation "Sprint Corporation") acquisition of Clearwire in 2013,[[77]](#cite_note-Meyer13-77)[[101]](#cite_note-King13-101) the carrier began using these frequencies for LTE service on networks built by [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung"), [Alcatel-Lucent](/wiki/Alcatel-Lucent "Alcatel-Lucent"), and [Nokia](/wiki/Nokia "Nokia").[[102]](#cite_note-Fitchard13a-102)[[103]](#cite_note-Reedy13-103)

As of March 2013, 156 commercial 4G LTE networks existed, including 142 LTE-FDD networks and 14 LTE-TDD networks.[[88]](#cite_note-Min13-88)
As of November 2013, the South Korean government planned to allow a fourth wireless carrier in 2014, which would provide LTE-TDD services,[[79]](#cite_note-Kim13-79) and in December 2013, LTE-TDD licenses were granted to China's three mobile operators, allowing commercial deployment of 4G LTE services.[[104]](#cite_note-Lai13-104)

In January 2014, Nokia Solutions and Networks indicated that it had completed a series of tests of [voice over LTE](#Voice_calls) ([VoLTE](#Voice_calls)) calls on China Mobile's TD-LTE network.[[105]](#cite_note-Munson14-105) The next month, Nokia Solutions and Networks and Sprint announced that they had demonstrated throughput speeds of 2.6 gigabits per second using a LTE-TDD network, surpassing the previous record of 1.6 gigabits per second.[[106]](#cite_note-TelecomTiger14-106)

## Features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LTE_(telecommunication)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Features")]

See also: [E-UTRA](/wiki/E-UTRA "E-UTRA")

Much of the LTE standard addresses the upgrading of 3G UMTS to what will eventually be [4G](/wiki/4G "4G") mobile communications technology. A large amount of the work is aimed at simplifying the architecture of the system, as it transitions from the existing UMTS [circuit](/wiki/Circuit_switching "Circuit switching") + [packet switching](/wiki/Packet_switching "Packet switching") combined network to an all-IP flat architecture system. [E-UTRA](/wiki/E-UTRA "E-UTRA") is the air interface of LTE. Its main features are:

* Peak download rates up to 299.6 Mbit/s and upload rates up to 75.4 Mbit/s depending on the [user equipment category](/wiki/E-UTRA#User_Equipment_(UE)_categories "E-UTRA") (with 4×4 antennas using 20 MHz of spectrum). Five different terminal classes have been defined from a voice-centric class up to a high-end terminal that supports the peak data rates. All terminals will be able to process 20 MHz bandwidth.
* Low data transfer latencies (sub-5 ms [latency](/wiki/Network_latency "Network latency") for small IP packets in optimal conditions), lower latencies for [handover](/wiki/Handover "Handover") and connection setup time than with previous [radio access technologies](/wiki/Radio_access_network "Radio access network").
* Improved support for mobility exemplified by support for terminals moving at up to 350 km/h (220 mph) or 500 km/h (310 mph) depending on the frequency
* [Orthogonal frequency-division multiple access](/wiki/Orthogonal_frequency-division_multiple_access "Orthogonal frequency-division multiple access") for the downlink, [Single-carrier FDMA](/wiki/Single-carrier_FDMA "Single-carrier FDMA") for the uplink to conserve power.
* Support for both [FDD](/wiki/Frequency-division_duplex "Frequency-division duplex") and [TDD](/wiki/Time-division_duplex "Time-division duplex") communication systems as well as half-duplex FDD with the same radio access technology.
* Support for all [frequency bands](/wiki/LTE_frequency_bands "LTE frequency bands") currently used by [IMT](/wiki/IMT-2000 "IMT-2000") systems by [ITU-R](/wiki/ITU-R "ITU-R").
* Increased spectrum flexibility: 1.4 MHz, 3 MHz, 5 MHz, 10 MHz, 15 MHz and 20 MHz wide cells are standardized. ([W-CDMA](/wiki/W-CDMA "W-CDMA") has no option for other than 5 MHz slices, leading to some problems rolling out in countries where 5 MHz is a commonly allocated width of the spectrum so would frequently already be in use with legacy standards such as [2G GSM](/wiki/GSM "GSM") and [cdmaOne](/wiki/IS-95 "IS-95").)
* Support for cell sizes from tens of metres radius ([femto](/wiki/Femtocell "Femtocell") and [picocells](/wiki/Picocell "Picocell")) up to 100 km (62 miles) radius [macrocells](/wiki/Macrocell "Macrocell"). In the lower frequency bands to be used in rural areas, 5 km (3.1 miles) is the optimal cell size, 30 km (19 miles) having reasonable performance, and up to 100 km cell sizes supported with acceptable performance. In the city and urban areas, higher frequency bands (such as 2.6 GHz in the EU) are used to support high-speed mobile broadband. In this case, cell sizes may be 1 km (0.62 miles) or even less.
* Support of at least 200 active data clients (connected users) in every 5 MHz cell.[[107]](#cite_note-107)
* Simplified architecture: The network side of [E-UTRAN](/wiki/E-UTRAN "E-UTRAN") is composed only of [eNode Bs](/wiki/ENode_B "ENode B").
* Support for inter-operation and co-existence with legacy standards (e.g., [GSM](/wiki/GSM "GSM")/[EDGE](/wiki/Enhanced_Data_Rates_for_GSM_Evolution "Enhanced Data Rates for GSM Evolution"), [UMTS](/wiki/Universal_Mobile_Telecommunications_System "Universal Mobile Telecommunications System") and [CDMA2000](/wiki/CDMA2000 "CDMA2000")). Users can start a call or transfer of data in an area using an LTE standard, and, should coverage be unavailable, continue the operation without any action on their part using GSM/[GPRS](/wiki/GPRS "GPRS") or W-CDMA-based UMTS or even [3GPP2](/wiki/3GPP2 "3GPP2") networks such as [cdmaOne](/wiki/CdmaOne "CdmaOne") or CDMA2000.
* Uplink and downlink [Carrier aggregation](/wiki/Carrier_aggregation "Carrier aggregation").
* [Packet-switched](/wiki/Packet_switching "Packet switching") radio interface.
* Support for MBSFN ([multicast-broadcast single-frequency network](/wiki/Multicast-broadcast_single-frequency_network "Multicast-broadcast single-frequency network")). This feature can deliver services such as Mobile TV using the LTE infrastructure and is a competitor for [DVB-H](/wiki/DVB-H "DVB-H")-based TV broadcast only LTE compatible devices receives LTE signal.

## Voice calls

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LTE_(telecommunication)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Voice calls")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/LTE-CSFB-E-UTRAN-UTRAN-GERAN-Interfaces.svg/500px-LTE-CSFB-E-UTRAN-UTRAN-GERAN-Interfaces.svg.png)](/wiki/File:LTE-CSFB-E-UTRAN-UTRAN-GERAN-Interfaces.svg)

cs domLTE CSFB to GSM/UMTS network interconnects

The LTE standard supports only [packet switching](/wiki/Packet_switching "Packet switching") with its all-IP network. Voice calls in GSM, UMTS, and CDMA2000 are [circuit switched](/wiki/Circuit_switching "Circuit switching"), so with the adoption of LTE, carriers will have to re-engineer their voice call network.[[108]](#cite_note-108) Four different approaches sprang up:

Voice over LTE (VoLTE)
:   Main article: [Voice over LTE](/wiki/Voice_over_LTE "Voice over LTE")

Circuit-switched fallback (CSFB)
:   In this approach, LTE just provides data services, and when a voice call is to be initiated or received, it will fall back to the circuit-switched domain. When using this solution, operators just need to upgrade the [MSC](/wiki/Mobile_Switching_Centre "Mobile Switching Centre") instead of deploying the [IMS](/wiki/IP_Multimedia_Subsystem "IP Multimedia Subsystem"), and therefore, can provide services quickly. However, the disadvantage is a longer call setup delay.

Simultaneous voice and LTE (SVLTE)
:   In this approach, the handset works simultaneously in the LTE and circuit-switched modes, with the LTE mode providing data services and the circuit-switched mode providing the voice service. This is a solution solely based on the handset, which does not have special requirements on the network and does not require the deployment of [IMS](/wiki/IP_Multimedia_Subsystem "IP Multimedia Subsystem") either. The disadvantage of this solution is that the phone can become expensive with high power consumption.

Single Radio Voice Call Continuity (SRVCC)
:   Main article: [SRVCC](/wiki/SRVCC "SRVCC")

One additional approach that is not initiated by operators is the usage of [over-the-top content](/wiki/Over-the-top_content "Over-the-top content") (OTT) services, using applications like [Skype](/wiki/Skype "Skype") and [Google Talk](/wiki/Google_Talk "Google Talk") to provide LTE voice service.[[109]](#cite_note-109)

Most major backers of LTE preferred and promoted VoLTE from the beginning. The lack of software support in initial LTE devices, as well as core network devices, however, led to a number of carriers promoting [VoLGA](/wiki/VoLGA "VoLGA") (Voice over LTE Generic Access) as an interim solution.[[110]](#cite_note-110) The idea was to use the same principles as [GAN](/wiki/Generic_Access_Network "Generic Access Network") (Generic Access Network, also known as UMA or Unlicensed Mobile Access), which defines the protocols through which a mobile handset can perform voice calls over a customer's private Internet connection, usually over wireless LAN. VoLGA however never gained much support, because VoLTE ([IMS](/wiki/IP_Multimedia_Subsystem "IP Multimedia Subsystem")) promises much more flexible services, albeit at the cost of having to upgrade the entire voice call infrastructure. VoLTE may require Single Radio Voice Call Continuity (SRVCC) to be able to smoothly perform a handover to a 2G or 3G network in case of poor LTE signal quality.[[111]](#cite_note-111)

While the industry has standardized on VoLTE, early LTE deployments required carriers to introduce circuit-switched fallback as a stopgap measure. When placing or receiving a voice call on a non-VoLTE-enabled network or device, LTE handsets will fall back to old 2G or 3G networks for the duration of the call.

### Enhanced voice quality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LTE_(telecommunication)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Enhanced voice quality")]

To ensure compatibility, 3GPP demands at least AMR-NB codec (narrow band), but the recommended speech codec for VoLTE is [Adaptive Multi-Rate Wideband](/wiki/Adaptive_Multi-Rate_Wideband "Adaptive Multi-Rate Wideband"), also known as [HD Voice](/wiki/HD_Voice "HD Voice"). This codec is mandated in 3GPP networks that support 16 kHz sampling.[[112]](#cite_note-112)

[Fraunhofer IIS](/wiki/Fraunhofer_Society "Fraunhofer Society") has proposed and demonstrated "Full-HD Voice", an implementation of the [AAC-ELD](/wiki/AAC-LD "AAC-LD") (Advanced Audio Coding – Enhanced Low Delay) codec for LTE handsets.[[113]](#cite_note-113) Where previous cell phone voice codecs only supported frequencies up to 3.5 kHz and upcoming [wideband audio](/wiki/Wideband_audio "Wideband audio") services branded as *HD Voice* up to 7 kHz, Full-HD Voice supports the entire bandwidth range from 20 Hz to 20 kHz. For end-to-end Full-HD Voice calls to succeed, however, both the caller's and recipient's handsets, as well as networks, have to support the feature.[[114]](#cite_note-114)

## Frequency bands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LTE_(telecommunication)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Frequency bands")]

See also: [LTE frequency bands](/wiki/LTE_frequency_bands "LTE frequency bands")

The LTE standard covers a range of many different bands, each of which is designated by both a frequency and a band number:

* North America – 600, 700, 850, 1700, 1900, 2300, 2500, 2600, 3500, 5000 MHz (bands 2, 4, 5, 7, 12, 13, 14, 17, 25, 26, 28, 29, 30, 38, 40, 41, 42, 43, 46, 48, 66, 71)
* Central America, South America and the Caribbean – 600, 700, 800, 850, 900, 1700, 1800, 1900, 2100, 2300, 2500, 2600, 3500, 5000 MHz (bands 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 12, 13, 14, 17, 20, 25, 26, 28, 29, 38, 40, 41, 42, 43, 46, 48, 66, 71)
* Europe – 450, 700, 800, 900, 1500, 1800, 2100, 2300, 2600, 3500, 3700 MHz (bands 1, 3, 7, 8, 20, 22, 28, 31, 32, 38, 40, 42, 43)[[115]](#cite_note-115)[[116]](#cite_note-116)
* Asia – 450, 700, 800, 850, 900, 1500, 1800, 1900, 2100, 2300, 2500, 2600, 3500 MHz (bands 1, 3, 5, 7, 8, 11, 18, 19, 20, 21, 26, 28, 31, 38, 39, 40, 41, 42)[[117]](#cite_note-117)
* Africa – 700, 800, 850, 900, 1800, 2100, 2300, 2500, 2600 MHz (bands 1, 3, 5, 7, 8, 20, 28, 40, 41)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Oceania (incl. Australia[[118]](#cite_note-118)[[119]](#cite_note-119) and New Zealand[[120]](#cite_note-120)) – 700, 850, 900, 1800, 2100, 2300, 2600 MHz (bands 1, 3, 5, 7, 8, 28, 40)

As a result, phones from one country may not work in other countries. Users will need a multi-band capable phone for roaming internationally.

## Patents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LTE_(telecommunication)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Patents")]

According to the [European Telecommunications Standards Institute](/wiki/European_Telecommunications_Standards_Institute "European Telecommunications Standards Institute")'s (ETSI) [intellectual property](/wiki/Intellectual_property "Intellectual property") rights (IPR) database, about 50 companies have declared, as of March 2012, holding [essential patents](/wiki/Essential_patent "Essential patent") covering the LTE standard.[[121]](#cite_note-ipeg-6-03-2012-121) The ETSI has made no investigation on the correctness of the declarations however,[[121]](#cite_note-ipeg-6-03-2012-121) so that "any analysis of essential LTE patents should take into account more than ETSI declarations."[[122]](#cite_note-122) Independent studies have found that about 3.3 to 5 percent of all revenues from handset manufacturers are spent on standard-essential patents. This is less than the combined published rates, due to reduced-rate licensing agreements, such as cross-licensing.[[123]](#cite_note-123)[[124]](#cite_note-124)[[125]](#cite_note-125)
